alert.title: Health Alert: Itchy Skin Rash Reported in Arba Minch Surroundings
alert.message: A cluster of 4 people in the Arba Minch area have reported unusual skin sores and intense itching. Health experts suspect an outbreak of Scabies. While this condition is treatable, it is highly contagious and can spread quickly through families and neighborhoods if people do not take precautions.
recommendations: Visit the nearest health center immediately if you or your children have an itchy rash or unusual skin bumps.
Do not share personal items like towels, clothing, or blankets with others.
If one person in your household is diagnosed, every family member should be checked by a health worker.
Avoid scratching the itchy areas, as this can lead to more serious skin infections.
alert.prevention_strategy: To stop the spread, practice good hygiene. Regularly wash your clothes and bedding in hot water and dry them in direct sunlight. Avoid direct skin-to-skin contact with anyone who has a visible rash or is known to be infected.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 4 cases appeared at once in the same area. This region has a history of skin outbreaks, and since the infection spreads very easily through close contact, we must act now to prevent it from reaching more families and schools.

alert.title: Health Alert: Outbreak of Itchy Skin Lesions in Arba Minch
alert.message: A cluster of 18 cases featuring unusual skin lesions and intense itching has been reported in the Arba Minch surroundings. Medical experts believe this is likely Scabies, a skin condition caused by tiny mites that spreads easily from person to person through close contact.
recommendations: Visit the nearest health center immediately if you or your family members have persistent itching or skin sores.
Wash all clothing, bedding, and towels used by affected individuals in hot, soapy water and dry them thoroughly in the sun.
Avoid sharing personal items like clothes, blankets, or towels with others.
If one person in a household is diagnosed, all family members should be treated at the same time to stop the spread.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to stay safe is to bathe regularly, wash your clothes and bedding often, and avoid close skin-to-skin contact with anyone showing symptoms of an itchy rash.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 18 people were affected in a short time in one area. Scabies is very contagious and can spread quickly through families, schools, and neighborhoods if it is not treated immediately.

alert.message: Health officials have identified 4 people in Turmi, South Omo, who are suffering from very high fevers and unusual bleeding spots on their skin. This is being treated as a high-priority health emergency because these symptoms suggest a serious viral illness that can spread quickly through the community.
recommendations: Seek medical help at the nearest health center immediately if you or a family member develops a sudden high fever.
Avoid touching the blood, saliva, or body fluids of anyone who is sick.
Report any new cases of high fever or skin bleeding to local health authorities right away.
If a person dies after having a fever, do not touch the body; wait for health professionals to handle the burial safely.
alert.prevention_strategy: Protect yourself from insect bites by wearing long sleeves and pants, using insect repellent, and sleeping under a treated mosquito net. Keep your home and animal areas clean, and avoid contact with sick people or animals.
alert.why_urgent: This is urgent because a cluster of 4 cases appeared at the same time in an area known for these types of outbreaks. The current weather and environment in Turmi make it very easy for the insects that carry these diseases to spread them to more people.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Cholera Outbreak at Konso Health Post
alert.message: A high-risk health alert has been issued for the Konso area. Five people have recently arrived at the health post suffering from severe vomiting and very watery diarrhea. Health experts are treating this as a Cholera outbreak. This disease is dangerous because it spreads quickly through a community and can make people very sick in a short amount of time.
recommendations: If you or a family member have watery diarrhea, go to the nearest health center immediately.
Start drinking Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) or plenty of clean fluids at the first sign of sickness.
Do not wait for symptoms to get better on their own; seek medical help right away.
Report any new cases of diarrhea in your neighborhood to local health workers.
alert.prevention_strategy: Keep your family safe by drinking only boiled or treated water. Always wash your hands with soap and clean water after using the toilet and before preparing or eating food. Ensure all food is cooked thoroughly and covered to keep flies away.
alert.why_urgent: This is urgent because five people got sick at the same time in an area where these diseases have happened before. Cholera can spread from person to person very fast through water and food, and it can be fatal if not treated immediately.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Diarrhea Outbreak at Konso Health Post
alert.message: Ten cases of severe watery diarrhea and vomiting have been reported at the Konso Health Post within the last 24 hours. These symptoms are signs of a potential Cholera outbreak. This is a serious health situation that requires immediate action to stop the disease from spreading to more families in our community.
recommendations: Go to the nearest health center immediately if you or a family member have watery diarrhea or vomiting.
Start drinking Oral Rehydration Salts (ORS) or clean fluids right away if symptoms begin.
Use only boiled or chemically treated water for drinking, cooking, and washing dishes.
Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and clean water after using the latrine and before preparing or eating food.
Ensure all food is cooked thoroughly and eaten while it is still hot.
alert.prevention_strategy: To stay safe, follow the 'Clean Water and Clean Hands' rule: Always treat your drinking water, keep your surroundings clean, and never eat raw or undercooked food. Promptly reporting new cases helps health workers stop the spread.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 10 people became very sick in a very short time. Because of current environmental conditions and limited access to clean water in the Konso Zone, this disease can spread extremely fast and become life-threatening if we do not act now.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Potential Yellow Fever Outbreak in Jinka
alert.message: Health officials have identified 5 cases of a serious illness in Jinka, South Omo Zone. Patients are reporting high-grade fever, severe headaches, and yellowing of the eyes and skin (jaundice). These symptoms strongly suggest an outbreak of Yellow Fever, a dangerous disease spread by mosquito bites.
recommendations: Go to the nearest health center immediately if you have a high fever, a bad headache, or yellowing of the eyes.
Report any sick family members or neighbors with these symptoms to local health workers right away.
Use mosquito nets every night and wear clothing that covers your arms and legs.
Cooperage with health teams who may be visiting your neighborhood for vaccinations or inspections.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to prevent Yellow Fever is to get vaccinated and avoid mosquito bites. Clear away any standing water in old tires, pots, or puddles near your home where mosquitoes breed. Always sleep under a treated bed net.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was issued because Yellow Fever can spread very quickly and can be fatal if not treated. Since Jinka has a history of these outbreaks and 5 people are already sick, we must act immediately to stop the disease from spreading to more people.

0  URGENT: Health alert from Jinka, South Omo Zone. We have 8 patients presenting with high-grade fever, severe headache, and jaundice (yellowing of eyes). 3 cases were lab-confirmed as Yellow Fever this morning, while the remaining 5 are considered probable based on clinical alignment. All patients are from the same village near the Omo River.
1                                                                          Konso Health Post: Cluster of 15 cases of acute watery diarrhea and severe vomiting detected in the last 24 hours. Initial investigation suggests a contaminated communal well. 10 cases are currently suspected and 5 are probable cholera. Immediate medical supplies needed.
2                                                                                            Turmi Update: 4 individuals in a nomadic group showing signs of high fever and hemorrhagic spots. These are currently suspected cases of an unidentified viral fever. Movement tracking is difficult due to their migratory patterns in the South Omo region.
3                                                                                             Arba Minch Surroundings: Local surveillance detected 22 cases of unusual skin lesions and itching among plantation workers. 18 are suspected and 4 have been confirmed by the regional clinic as a localized fungal outbreak exacerbated by recent flooding.

additional_info.source: same village near the Omo River

issues: Geographic plausibility is high: South Omo Zone and areas near the Omo River are known endemic foci for Yellow Fever in Ethiopia.
Clinical presentation (fever, headache, jaundice) is a classic match for the toxic phase of Yellow Fever.

Temporal anomaly: 'Lab-confirmed' status provided 'this morning' for cases just reported is exceptionally fast for a remote region like Jinka, unless the patients were under investigation for several days prior.
Missing demographic data: Age, sex, and occupation (e.g., forest workers vs. farmers) are critical for distinguishing between sylvan and urban transmission cycles.
Missing vaccination status: Information on whether these individuals were part of previous mass vaccination campaigns in the South Omo Zone is vital for validation.

risk_analysis.reason: The clinical triad of high-grade fever, severe headache, and jaundice (yellowing of eyes) is highly indicative of Yellow Fever, which is endemic to the South Omo Zone. A cluster of 3 cases appearing within a single morning suggests a localized outbreak of a high-consequence pathogen. While waterborne Hepatitis E or severe Malaria are possibilities given the regional context, the severity and rapid onset of symptoms in an endemic zone for viral hemorrhagic fevers warrant a high risk rating.

agent_opinions.reason: The clinical triad of high-grade fever, severe headache, and jaundice (yellowing of eyes) is highly indicative of Yellow Fever, which is endemic to the South Omo Zone. A cluster of 3 cases appearing within a single morning suggests a localized outbreak of a high-consequence pathogen. While waterborne Hepatitis E or severe Malaria are possibilities given the regional context, the severity and rapid onset of symptoms in an endemic zone for viral hemorrhagic fevers warrant a high risk rating.

From a statistical perspective, the appearance of 3 cases with severe clinical symptoms (jaundice and high fever) within a single morning indicates an acute cluster with a high rate of increase. Jinka's role as a major administrative hub for the South Omo Zone increases the risk of rapid transmission due to population density and mobility. Historically, this region is a known endemic zone for Yellow Fever; the detection of a cluster of this size in such a short timeframe deviates significantly from the sporadic historical baseline of 1-5 cases for other illnesses, suggesting a potent outbreak source.

The South Omo Zone is a historically recognized high-risk area for Yellow Fever (YF) in Ethiopia. The specific combination of high-grade fever and jaundice (yellowing of eyes) is a classic clinical indicator for YF or Viral Hemorrhagic Fevers. While the historical data summary provided focuses on recent respiratory and enteric issues, the South Omo region's ecological profile (tropical highland/lowland interface) has a documented history of sylvatic and urban YF cycles. Given the severity of symptoms and the regional history, a cluster of 3 cases is considered a high-risk sentinel event.

agent_opinions.possible_disease: Yellow Fever or Viral Hepatitis (Hepatitis E)
agent_opinions.reason: The symptoms of jaundice and high-grade fever in a cluster are highly concerning. From an environmental perspective, Jinka's 'inconsistent' water quality and poor sanitation infrastructure provide a high-risk environment for waterborne pathogens like Hepatitis E. Additionally, the tropical highland climate (16-27°C) is conducive to Aedes mosquito breeding, making Yellow Fever a primary suspect. The current administrative transition to the South Ethiopia Regional State may also impact the speed of the public health response and maintenance of water systems, increasing the risk of a localized outbreak escalating.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Suspected Yellow Fever in Jinka
alert.message: Health officials have identified three cases of a serious illness in Jinka, South Omo Zone. Patients are suffering from very high fevers, severe headaches, and yellowing of the eyes and skin (jaundice). These signs strongly suggest a possible outbreak of Yellow Fever, a dangerous disease spread by mosquito bites.
recommendations: If you or someone you know has a high fever and yellow eyes, go to the nearest health center immediately.
Tell your local health worker if you see neighbors or family members with these symptoms.
Do not attempt to treat these symptoms with home remedies; seek professional medical help.
If you are feeling sick, sleep under a mosquito net to prevent mosquitoes from biting you and spreading the virus to others.
alert.prevention_strategy: The most effective way to stay safe is to get the Yellow Fever vaccine. You can also protect yourself by wearing long-sleeved clothes, using mosquito repellent, and emptying containers of standing water around your home where mosquitoes lay their eggs.
alert.why_urgent: This situation is high risk because the South Omo Zone has seen this disease before, and it can spread very quickly through local mosquitoes. Because the symptoms are severe and can lead to death, we must act now to stop it from spreading to more people.

alert.title: Health Alert: Sudden Increase in Fever and Joint Pain in Arba Minch
alert.message: We have detected 15 cases of people working in Arba Minch banana plantations suffering from unusual insect bites and severe joint pain. These symptoms point to Chikungunya Fever, a disease carried by mosquitoes. Because many people in the same area are getting sick at once, we need to take action to stop it from spreading further to other workers and families.
recommendations: Go to the nearest health center immediately if you have a fever or painful joints.
Rest as much as possible and drink plenty of clean water if you feel unwell.
Tell your coworkers and neighbors to watch for these symptoms.
Do not take medicine without a doctor's advice, as some painkillers can be dangerous depending on the illness.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to stay safe is to avoid mosquito bites. Wear long-sleeved shirts and long trousers while working in the plantation. Use mosquito nets when sleeping and empty out any containers with standing water where mosquitoes like to lay their eggs.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 15 people in a small area got sick very quickly. Arba Minch's warm environment and the plantation conditions make it easy for mosquitoes to spread this disease fast if we don't protect ourselves now.

consensus.final_reasoning: The clinical triad of localized 'unusual insect bites' (likely representing a pruritic rash or vector exposure) combined with joint pain (arthralgia) is a classic presentation for alphaviral infections. Given the location in a banana plantation in Ethiopia—where Aedes and Anopheles mosquito vectors are prevalent—Chikungunya or O'nyong-nyong fever are the most probable matches. A cluster of 15 cases indicates an active transmission cycle requiring immediate vector control.

While the case count is currently low (3), the severity of the symptoms suggests either critical environmental exposure or an acute reaction to contaminated water sources, which are reported issues in the Awash basin. This differs significantly from the respiratory pattern seen in recent regional historical data.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Illness Cases at Semera Port
alert.message: Three people at Semera Port have recently collapsed and experienced severe vomiting. This may be caused by the extreme heat in the area or a waterborne illness (Acute Watery Diarrhea). We are monitoring the situation closely to protect everyone at the port.
recommendations: Seek medical help immediately if you feel dizzy, extremely hot, or start vomiting.
Drink plenty of clean, safe water throughout the day, even if you are not thirsty.
If someone collapses, move them to a cool, shaded area and call for help right away.
Report any similar symptoms to port health officers or supervisors immediately.
alert.prevention_strategy: Stay cool and stay clean: Drink safe water, rest in the shade during the hottest parts of the day, and always wash your hands with soap and clean water before eating or touching your face.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was issued because the high temperatures in the Afar region can cause life-threatening heatstroke, and symptoms like vomiting can spread quickly if they are caused by a germ in the water or food.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Suspected Measles Outbreak in Jijiga Displacement Camp
alert.message: Five cases of suspected measles have been reported in a displacement camp in the Jijiga region. Measles is a very contagious disease that causes high fever and a red rash. Because the camp is crowded, the virus can spread very quickly from person to person. Health teams are working to confirm the cases and stop the spread.
recommendations: Take any child with a fever and a rash to the nearest health center immediately.
Keep sick children away from other people, especially babies and pregnant women.
Ensure all children in your family have received their measles vaccinations.
Cover your mouth and nose when coughing or sneezing.
Wash your hands often with soap and clean water.
alert.prevention_strategy: The most effective way to prevent measles is through vaccination. Two doses of the measles vaccine provide lifelong protection. In crowded areas, keeping living spaces clean and well-ventilated also helps reduce the risk of infection.
alert.why_urgent: This is urgent because measles is one of the most contagious diseases in the world. In a displacement camp where many people live close together, even 5 cases can quickly turn into a massive outbreak that puts many children's lives at risk.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Increase in Stomach Illnesses in Bahir Dar and Lake Tana Area
alert.message: Health officials have identified 8 cases of severe stomach illness near the Lake Tana area. Patients are suffering from intense nausea and abdominal pain. This cluster of cases suggests a waterborne infection, such as Cholera or Typhoid Fever, which can spread quickly through contaminated water and food.
recommendations: If you experience severe stomach pain, vomiting, or diarrhea, go to the nearest health center immediately.
Only drink water that has been boiled for at least one minute or treated with water purification tablets.
Wash your hands thoroughly with soap and clean water after using the latrine and before preparing or eating food.
Avoid eating raw vegetables or unpeeled fruits; ensure all food is cooked thoroughly and served hot.
Avoid swimming or bathing directly in Lake Tana until further notice.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to stay safe is the 'Boil, Wash, and Cook' method: Always boil your drinking water, wash your hands frequently with soap, and ensure all food is freshly cooked and handled with clean hands.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was issued because 8 people getting sick at the same time in one area is a warning sign of an outbreak. Because Lake Tana is a major water source, the risk of the illness spreading to more people is high unless we take action now.

alert.title: Health Alert: Possible Measles Outbreak in Hawassa
alert.message: Health officials have identified 12 cases of suspected Measles (Rubeola) in Hawassa. Patients are reporting high fever and skin rashes. Because this many cases have appeared at once, there is a medium risk that the illness could spread further into the community if precautions are not taken immediately.
recommendations: Seek medical help immediately at the nearest health center if you or your child develop a high fever and a rash.
Keep children with symptoms at home and away from school or public places to prevent the virus from spreading.
Check your family's immunization records to ensure everyone has received their measles vaccinations.
Practice good hygiene by washing hands frequently with soap and water.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to prevent Measles is through vaccination. Ensure all children receive their two doses of the vaccine. Additionally, avoid crowded places and keep living areas well-ventilated to reduce the chance of the virus spreading through the air.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 12 cases in one area indicate the virus is actively moving through the community. Measles is highly contagious, and Hawassa’s population density makes it easy for the disease to spread quickly to many families if we don't act now.

2                              Bahir Dar update - Lake Tana area: 8 fishermen showing signs of severe nausea and abdominal pain. They mentioned a strange smell coming from the water near the northern shore. Local clinic is overwhelmed.

consensus.final_reasoning: The clinical presentation of high fever paired with skin rashes in a cluster of 12 cases is highly characteristic of Measles, which remains endemic in parts of Ethiopia. While the symptoms could also align with vector-borne diseases like Dengue or Chikungunya given the mild tropical climate and proximity to Lake Hawassa, the rapid appearance of a rash-fever cluster in an urban center typically suggests a highly contagious viral exanthem. The risk is marked as Medium because while the case count is currently low compared to historical respiratory data, the potential for rapid transmission in a densely populated urban area like Hawassa is significant.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Respiratory Outbreak in Mercato, Addis Ababa
alert.message: Health officials have identified a high-risk outbreak of respiratory illness in the Mercato area. So far, 45 people have reported symptoms including a dry cough and serious trouble breathing. This appears to be a fast-spreading viral pneumonia, similar to COVID-19 or a strong flu, and it requires immediate attention to keep the community safe.
recommendations: Wear a well-fitting face mask at all times when in public areas or markets.
If you have a dry cough or find it hard to breathe, go to the nearest health center immediately.
Avoid crowded places and keep at least two arm-lengths of distance from others.
Wash your hands with soap and water frequently, especially after touching surfaces in public.
Stay home if you feel unwell or have any symptoms of a cold or flu.
alert.prevention_strategy: The best way to stay safe is to stop germs from moving between people. Cover your mouth and nose with a mask, keep your hands clean, and avoid close contact with anyone who is coughing or sneezing.
alert.why_urgent: This is urgent because 45 cases have appeared very quickly in a short time. Mercato is one of the most crowded areas in the city, which means the sickness can spread to hundreds of people very fast if we do not act now.

alert.title: URGENT HEALTH ALERT: Respiratory Illness Outbreak in Mercato
alert.message: A high-risk health alert has been issued for the Mercato area of Addis Ababa. In the last 48 hours, 45 people have fallen ill with severe respiratory symptoms, including dry coughs and difficulty breathing. Health experts believe this is a highly contagious viral pneumonia, similar to COVID-19 or a strong flu variant, that is spreading quickly through the community.
recommendations: Seek medical attention immediately if you have a persistent dry cough or trouble breathing.
Wear a face mask at all times when in public, especially in crowded market areas.
Avoid the Mercato area for the next few days unless it is absolutely necessary.
Wash your hands with soap and water frequently or use hand sanitizer.
If you feel sick, stay home and keep away from family members to stop the spread.
alert.prevention_strategy: To stay safe, keep a distance of at least two meters from others, wear a mask over your nose and mouth, and avoid touching your face. Keeping your hands clean and staying away from crowded spots are the best ways to protect yourself and your family.
alert.why_urgent: This alert was generated because 45 people got sick in a very short time in one of the most crowded areas of the city. Because so many people visit Mercato every day, there is a high risk that this illness could spread across all of Addis Ababa very quickly if we do not act now.
